summary:
Bariatric Considerations

Over 40% of U.S. adults are classified as having obesity (BMI ≥ 30), and the prevalence of severe obesity (BMI ≥ 40 or Class III obesity) continues to rise. Bariatric patients — those with significant obesity — present unique challenges in prehospital care, including altered physiology, difficult airway management, equipment limitations, and transport logistics. This section covers the essential knowledge and skills for providing safe, effective, and dignified care to bariatric patients.

Definition: For EMS purposes, a bariatric patient is typically defined as a patient with a BMI > 30 OR a patient weighing approximately > 300 lbs (136 kg) — though these thresholds vary by equipment capacity. The term "bariatric" comes from the Greek words baros (weight) and iatrikos (medical).

Epidemiology and Physiologic Changes

Obesity is a chronic, progressive disease that affects virtually every organ system. Understanding the pathophysiology of obesity is essential to anticipating and managing the complications that arise in bariatric patients:

SystemPathophysiologyClinical Implications for EMS
RespiratoryRestrictive lung disease from increased abdominal mass pushing up on the diaphragm; decreased chest wall compliance; reduced functional residual capacity (FRC); obstructive sleep apnea (OSA); obesity hypoventilation syndrome (Pickwickian syndrome — chronic hypoxemia and hypercapnia)Rapid desaturation during apnea due to low FRC and increased oxygen consumption; difficult BVM ventilation due to poor mask seal and high resistance; increased risk of aspiration; chronic hypoxemia may alter baseline SpO₂; CPAP/BiPAP may be needed for severe OSA
CardiovascularIncreased total blood volume and cardiac output (chronic volume overload); left ventricular hypertrophy; decreased cardiac reserve; hypertension; accelerated atherosclerosis; increased risk of pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure (cor pulmonale)Higher baseline BP and HR; limited ability to compensate for hypovolemia or sepsis; higher risk of CHF exacerbation; ECG may show LVH (voltage criteria) and non-specific ST changes; increased risk of PE and DVT
Airway/AnatomyLarge neck circumference (>17 in / 43 cm in men = difficult intubation marker); redundant pharyngeal tissue (false vocal cords, arytenoids); limited neck extension (large posterior cervical fat pad — "buffalo hump"); large tongue (macroglossia); narrow oropharyngeal space; increased Mallampati scoreDifficult airway management — poor laryngoscopy view; difficult mask seal; rapid desaturation; BVM ventilation may be ineffective without proper positioning; supraglottic airways may be easier than intubation
GastrointestinalIncreased intra-abdominal pressure; high prevalence of gastroesophageal reflux (GERD) and hiatal hernia; delayed gastric emptying; larger gastric volume; increased risk of aspirationIncreased aspiration risk — maintain suction readiness; consider rapid sequence intubation positioning; gastric decompression may help
Metabolic/EndocrineInsulin resistance → type 2 diabetes; metabolic syndrome (hyperlipidemia, hypertension, insulin resistance); chronic inflammation (elevated inflammatory markers); altered drug metabolismHigher prevalence of diabetic emergencies; altered medication pharmacokinetics; increased risk of metabolic emergencies
MusculoskeletalDegenerative joint disease (osteoarthritis of knees, hips, spine); chronic low back pain; limited mobility; muscle deconditioningDifficulty positioning for transport and assessment; chronic pain may complicate acute pain assessment; risk of further injury during lifting and moving
IntegumentaryIntertrigo (skin breakdown in skin folds); lymphedema; chronic venous stasis; increased risk of cellulitis and pressure ulcers; fragile skinAssess skin folds for rashes, infections, and wounds; careful handling to avoid skin tears; increased risk of pressure ulcers from long transports on hard surfaces
Clinical Pearl — Oxygenation: Bariatric patients have a reduced functional residual capacity (FRC) and increased metabolic oxygen demand. This means they desaturate significantly faster during apnea than normal-weight patients — sometimes in less than 1-2 minutes. Pre-oxygenate aggressively (100% O₂ via non-rebreather for 3-5 minutes) and be prepared to intervene early if oxygenation drops.

Equipment Challenges

Standard EMS equipment is often inadequate for bariatric patients. Anticipating these limitations and having the appropriate equipment available is critical:

  • Extra-large BP cuffs: Standard adult cuffs (12-13 cm width) are too small for arms with a circumference > 32 cm. Using a cuff that is too small produces falsely elevated blood pressure readings (up to 10-50 mmHg too high). Use a bariatric/thigh cuff or measure forearm BP with a appropriately sized cuff if the arm circumference exceeds the maximum for the largest cuff available.
  • Longer needles and IO catheters: Standard IV catheters may be too short to access veins in patients with significant subcutaneous tissue. For peripheral IV access, consider longer catheters (2-2.5 inches) or ultrasound guidance if available. For intraosseous access, use the longer IO needles designed for bariatric patients (typically 25-45 mm for the proximal tibia, longer for the humeral head). The sternum is often a better IO site in bariatric patients due to thinner overlying tissue.
  • Reinforced stretchers and cots: Standard ambulance cots have weight limits (typically 350-500 lbs). Know your equipment's capacity. Use bariatric-rated cots, stretchers, and backboards rated for higher weight limits. Some services have specialized bariatric transport units.
  • Narrow doorways and hallways: Bariatric patients may not fit through standard residential doorways (typically 32-36 inches). A bariatric transport chair or stair chair may not fit. Plan ahead — have fire department or additional personnel available for creative extrication (removing doors from hinges, using a full-length backboard to slide the patient, or evacuating through a window if necessary).
  • Blood pressure measurement: If the arm circumference exceeds the largest BP cuff available, use the forearm (with the radial artery at wrist level) or the calf (if it fits a large cuff). Document the alternative site on the PCR.
  • ECG monitoring: Standard ECG electrodes may not adhere well to skin folds or diaphoretic skin. Consider using extended-life electrodes, prepping the skin with alcohol and drying it, or using alternative electrode placement. Electrode placement may need to be modified if the chest anatomy prevents standard precordial lead positioning.

Clinical Challenges

Difficult IV Access

Peripheral IV access is frequently challenging in bariatric patients due to:

  • Deep or non-visible veins obscured by subcutaneous tissue
  • Higher failure rate with standard catheters (catheter may not be long enough to reach the vein)
  • Higher rates of infiltration due to poor tissue turgor and fragile veins
  • Increased skin-to-vein distance

Strategies: Use longer catheters (2-2.5 inches). Consider the external jugular vein (if accessible and permitted by your protocol). Use ultrasound guidance if available. Do not delay transport or critical interventions (epinephrine, naloxone, dextrose) for IV access — these can be given IM or IO. Have a low threshold for IO access if IV fails after 1-2 attempts.

Difficult Airway Management

The bariatric airway presents multiple overlapping challenges that make it one of the most difficult scenarios in prehospital care:

  • Mask ventilation: Poor mask seal due to facial adiposity and large cheeks; high resistance to ventilation from chest wall mass and abdominal pressure; gastric insufflation is more likely due to high ventilation pressures.
  • Supraglottic airway devices: May be easier than intubation. Choose the correct size — too small will leak, too large will cause trauma. Second-generation supraglottic airways with gastric access ports are preferred.
  • Intubation: Poor laryngoscopic view (Mallampati III-IV common), limited neck extension, anterior larynx, limited mouth opening. The STOP-BANG questionnaire is a screening tool for OSA but not typically used in the field — instead, recognize that the bariatric airway is by definition a potentially difficult airway and have backup plans ready.
  • Rapid desaturation: As noted above, desaturation during apnea can occur within 1-2 minutes due to low FRC and high metabolic rate. Pre-oxygenate with 100% O₂ for 3-5 minutes before any airway manipulation. Consider apneic oxygenation (15 L/min O₂ via nasal cannula during laryngoscopy).
Positioning for Airway — The Ramped Position

The ramped position is the recommended positioning for bariatric patients requiring airway management or ventilatory support. It is critical to understand that the standard sniffing position does not work in bariatric patients due to the large posterior chest and shoulder mass.

Ramped Position — How to Achieve It:
Place folded towels, blankets, or commercial ramps under the patient's upper back, shoulders, and head so that the external auditory meatus (ear) is horizontally aligned with the sternal notch. This creates a "ramp" that elevates the head and shoulders above the chest. In this position:
- The larynx is naturally aligned for visualization
- BVM ventilation is more effective (less resistance from chest mass)
- CPAP/BiPAP seals better and is more comfortable
- The risk of passive regurgitation and aspiration is reduced

NREMT Key Point: The ramped position (ear-to-sternal-notch alignment) is critical for successful BVM ventilation and intubation in bariatric patients. Do not attempt to place a bariatric patient flat — doing so will cause rapid desaturation from mechanical compression of the diaphragm by the abdominal contents.
Acoustic Windows and Auscultation

Bariatric patients present challenges for physical examination:

  • Lung sounds: Thick chest wall and adipose tissue attenuate sound transmission. Breath sounds, heart sounds, and bowel sounds may all be quieter than expected. You may need to listen in the axillae rather than the traditional anterior chest locations. Wheezes, crackles, and other adventitious sounds may be harder to appreciate.
  • Heart sounds: Often distant or muffled. Do not rely on the absence of audible murmurs or rubs to rule out cardiac pathology.
  • Palpation: Abdominal palpation is limited by the abdominal wall thickness. Abscesses, masses, and organomegaly may be non-palpable. Guarding and rebound may not be appreciable despite significant intra-abdominal pathology.

Medication Considerations

Drug distribution, metabolism, and clearance are altered in bariatric patients. While detailed pharmacology is beyond the EMT scope, understanding the basic principles helps anticipate clinical response:

  • Lipophilic drugs (e.g., benzodiazepines, fentanyl, propofol, barbiturates) distribute extensively into adipose tissue, increasing the volume of distribution (Vd). This means a higher initial dose (loading dose) may be needed to achieve therapeutic serum levels, but the drug accumulates in fat and has a prolonged half-life as it slowly redistributes back into the circulation.
  • Hydrophilic drugs (e.g., neuromuscular blockers, aminoglycosides, beta-blockers) distribute less into fat. The volume of distribution may be closer to lean body weight. Dosing based on IBW (ideal body weight) is common for these drugs.
  • Weight-based medications: For drugs dosed by weight (e.g., epinephrine, naloxone, dextrose 50%), use the patient's actual body weight. For drugs with narrow therapeutic windows, be aware that the recommended dose may be adjusted based on specific protocols.
  • Altered clearance: Hepatic and renal clearance may be increased (due to increased organ mass and blood flow) or decreased (due to fatty infiltration of the liver and decreased renal function from comorbidities). This variability makes drug response less predictable.
  • EMS provider implications: Do not change drug doses without specific protocol guidance. Be aware that bariatric patients may require more or less sedation than expected. Monitor for prolonged effects from benzodiazepines and opiates — they may accumulate in adipose tissue and cause delayed respiratory depression.
Clinical Pearl: When administering IM medications (epinephrine, glucagon, naloxone), use a longer needle (1.5-2 inches) to ensure intramuscular delivery rather than subcutaneous injection into adipose tissue. The preferred site is the anterolateral thigh — even in bariatric patients, this provides a large muscle mass with reliable absorption.

Transport Considerations

Transporting a bariatric patient requires planning, adequate personnel, and specialized equipment. Inadequate preparation can lead to patient injury, EMS provider injury, and significant delays:

  • Team size: Proper lifting and moving of a bariatric patient typically requires 4-6+ personnel. Determine this number BEFORE attempting to lift. Use a mechanical lift or air-assisted lift device whenever available. Calling for additional resources early (fire department, second ambulance) is better than struggling and risking injury.
  • Bariatric-specific equipment: Bariatric stretcher (higher width and weight capacity), bariatric stair chair, reinforced backboard or scoop stretcher, wide ambulance cot with power-load capability, and wide-door or bariatric ambulance for larger patients.
  • Patient dignity and psychological care: Bariatric patients often experience embarrassment, stigma, and anxiety during EMS encounters. Be aware that previous healthcare experiences may have been negative. Use respectful, non-judgmental language. Do not make comments about the patient's weight — even in an attempt to "joke" or "relax" the patient. Cover the patient appropriately during transport. Use adequate-sized hospital gowns and sheets. Explain what is happening and why extra personnel or equipment may be needed without making the patient feel like a burden.
  • Mechanical lifts: When available, use powered mechanical lifts (e.g., Hoyer lift, ceiling-mounted lifts, or air-assisted transfer mattresses) to move bariatric patients. These reduce the risk of injury to both patient and EMS providers. If not available, use a draw sheet and log-roll technique with sufficient personnel.
  • Access challenges: Assess the scene for obstacles before moving the patient. Narrow doorways, stairs, small elevators, and tight corners may all present barriers. Have a plan B if the primary egress does not accommodate the patient.
  • Seatbelt considerations: Standard ambulance cot seatbelts and stretcher straps may be too short to secure a bariatric patient. Use extenders or longer straps. Secure the patient properly before transport — an unrestrained bariatric patient in a moving ambulance poses a safety risk to themselves and the crew.
  • Vehicular considerations: Some bariatric patients may exceed the safe working load of standard ambulance cots or the cot fastener system. Know your vehicle's capacity. Consider using a bariatric transport vehicle if available for very large patients.
NREMT Key Point: The ramped position (ear-to-sternal-notch alignment) is the single most important modification for managing the bariatric airway. The standard sniffing position does NOT work in bariatric patients — the large posterior chest and shoulder mass prevent proper neck extension and laryngeal alignment. Elevate the head and upper body to create a horizontal ear-sternal notch line before attempting BVM ventilation or intubation. Failure to do so will result in rapid desaturation and failed airway management.

quiz.q: A 52-year-old male with a BMI of 48 requires BVM ventilation. He is supine on the stretcher. What is the MOST important modification to improve ventilation?
options: Use a smaller mask for a better seal
Place him in the ramped position (elevate head and upper body so ear is aligned with sternal notch)
Increase the ventilation rate to 20 breaths/min
Place him in Trendelenburg position

quiz.q: An EMS provider attempts to take a blood pressure on a bariatric patient using a standard adult cuff. The reading is 168/94. What is the MOST likely explanation?
options: The patient is hypertensive and needs emergency treatment
A standard cuff is too small for a large arm — this causes falsely elevated readings
The patient has white-coat hypertension from anxiety
The reading is accurate — standard cuffs work on all patients

quiz.q: A 48-year-old female weighing 320 lbs requires IV access for medication administration. Two attempts at peripheral IV access have failed. What is the BEST next step?
options: Continue attempting peripheral IV access until successful
Consider IO access using a longer bariatric IO needle, or administer the medication IM with a longer needle if appropriate
Abandon IV access and transport without medication
Use a butterfly needle to access a superficial vein

quiz.q: A bariatric patient with obstructive sleep apnea is being transported for respiratory distress. What position optimizes the patient's spontaneous breathing during transport?
options: Supine flat
Sitting upright with head and shoulders elevated (ramped position)
Left lateral recumbent
Trendelenburg

quiz.q: An EMS provider needs to administer IM epinephrine to a bariatric patient in anaphylaxis. What needle length is MOST appropriate?
options: Standard 5/8-inch needle (it will reach the muscle in most patients)
A longer needle (1.5-2 inches) to ensure intramuscular delivery through the adipose tissue
Use an insulin syringe to draw up the epinephrine — it has a finer needle
Any needle length is fine — the epinephrine will be absorbed regardless

quiz.q: HARD: A bariatric patient with a BMI of 52 requires intubation. Pre-oxygenation has been attempted with a non-rebreather mask. The patient's SpO₂ drops from 95% to 80% within 90 seconds of the first laryngoscopy attempt. What is the MOST likely explanation for this rapid desaturation?
options: The patient was not pre-oxygenated long enough

The patient has low functional residual capacity (FRC) and high metabolic oxygen demand due to obesity — desaturation in under 2 minutes is expected. Aggressive pre-oxygenation and apneic oxygenation are critical
The patient has a pneumothorax
The pulse oximeter is malfunctioning

quiz.q: HARD: An EMS provider is assessing a bariatric patient with abdominal pain. The patient has a large abdominal girth and thick abdominal wall. Which of the following assessment limitations should the EMS provider anticipate?
options: The abdominal exam will be easier because the abdominal muscles are more relaxed

Abdominal palpation may be unreliable — guarding, rebound, and masses may not be appreciable despite significant intra-abdominal pathology. Auscultation of bowel and lung sounds may also be diminished
Bowel sounds will be louder due to the increased abdominal mass
The abdominal exam is always reliable regardless of body habitus

flashcards.question: What is the ramped position and why is it essential for bariatric airway management?
flashcards.answer: The ramped position elevates the head and upper body so the external auditory meatus (ear) is horizontally aligned with the sternal notch. This optimizes laryngeal view, improves BVM ventilation, and reduces aspiration risk. Standard sniffing position does NOT work in bariatric patients.

flashcards.question: Why do bariatric patients desaturate more rapidly during apnea?
flashcards.answer: Due to reduced functional residual capacity (FRC) from abdominal mass pushing up the diaphragm, increased chest wall resistance, AND increased metabolic oxygen consumption. Desaturation can occur within 1-2 minutes of apnea. Pre-oxygenate aggressively with 100% O₂ for 3-5 minutes.

flashcards.question: What equipment adaptations are needed for bariatric patient care?
flashcards.answer: Extra-large BP cuffs (standard cuffs give falsely high readings), longer IV catheters (2-2.5 in), longer IO needles (25-45 mm), reinforced bariatric stretchers/cots, wider stair chairs, longer seatbelt extenders, and bariatric transport vehicles for very large patients.

flashcards.question: Why is IV access more difficult in bariatric patients and what are the alternatives?
flashcards.answer: Due to deeper/non-visible veins from increased subcutaneous tissue. Use longer catheters (2-2.5 in), consider external jugular vein, or use ultrasound. Have a low threshold for IO access (use longer IO needles). Do NOT delay critical medications — give IM or IO if IV fails.

flashcards.question: How does obesity affect medication distribution in the body?
flashcards.answer: Lipophilic drugs (benzodiazepines, fentanyl, propofol) distribute into adipose tissue — higher volume of distribution (higher loading dose needed) and prolonged half-life (accumulation in fat). Hydrophilic drugs (neuromuscular blockers) distribute less into fat. Clearance may be altered.

flashcards.question: What is the recommended team size for moving a bariatric patient?
flashcards.answer: Typically 4-6+ personnel. Use mechanical lifts or air-assisted devices when available. Call for additional resources early — fire department or second ambulance. Proper lifting techniques are essential to prevent EMS provider injury.

flashcards.question: What are the auscultation challenges in bariatric patients?
flashcards.answer: Thick chest wall and adipose tissue attenuate breath sounds, heart sounds, and bowel sounds. Listen in the axillae rather than the anterior chest. Absence of audible adventitious sounds does NOT rule out pathology. Lung pathology may be harder to detect.

flashcards.question: How should an EMS provider approach IM medication administration in a bariatric patient?
flashcards.answer: Use a longer needle (1.5-2 inches) to ensure intramuscular delivery rather than subcutaneous injection into adipose tissue. The preferred site is the anterolateral thigh — it provides the largest muscle mass with reliable absorption regardless of body habitus.

critical.scenario: You and your partner respond to a home for a 55-year-old male with difficulty breathing. The patient weighs approximately 400 lbs (estimated BMI 55). He is sitting upright in a chair, using accessory muscles to breathe, and can only speak 2-3 words between breaths. His SpO₂ is 86% on room air. The patient has a history of CHF, hypertension, and severe obstructive sleep apnea. He uses CPAP at home but the machine is not working today. His wife is anxious and asking if you can help him. The patient lives in a second-floor walk-up apartment with narrow, winding stairs. The doorway to the apartment is a standard 32-inch door. Your ambulance has a standard cot (rated to 350 lbs) and standard equipment.
critical.question: This patient is in severe respiratory distress and needs immediate intervention and transport. He is also a bariatric patient with equipment and access challenges. What is the EMS provider's optimal management sequence?
options: Start BVM ventilation immediately and try to get him down the stairs as fast as possible

Apply high-flow oxygen, position him upright (ramped position with blankets behind his head and shoulders), start CPAP if available, call for additional personnel (fire department) for lifting assistance and a bariatric-capable ambulance if available. If his SpO₂ does not improve or he deteriorates, begin BVM ventilation. Plan the extrication route carefully — the standard stair chair may not fit through doorways; consider using a reinforced backboard and slide him down stairs with sufficient personnel. Protect the patient's dignity throughout — do not discuss his weight in front of him
Refuse transport since your cot cannot hold his weight — tell the wife to call a bariatric transport service
Transport him in the standard cot despite the weight limit — it will probably hold him for this one trip

critical.scenario: You are treating a 48-year-old female with a BMI of 46 who is experiencing a severe asthma exacerbation. She has diffuse expiratory wheezing, an SpO₂ of 88%, and a respiratory rate of 30. She is sitting upright and leaning forward. You administer nebulized albuterol and apply oxygen via non-rebreather. After 10 minutes, the patient shows minimal improvement — her SpO₂ is 90%, wheezing is still loud, and she is becoming fatigued. You decide to assist her ventilations with a BVM. Your partner attempts to place the mask on her face but cannot get a seal — the mask is too small and air is leaking around the cheeks. The patient becomes more distressed and her SpO₂ drops to 84%.
critical.question: You are unable to effectively ventilate the patient due to the mask seal problem. What are your alternatives and next steps?
options: Continue trying to hold the mask tighter — the seal will eventually work

Use a two-person BVM technique (one person holds the mask with two hands for a better seal, the second person squeezes the bag). Position the patient in the ramped position to improve airway alignment. If still ineffective, consider a supraglottic airway device if permitted by protocol. Request ALS backup for advanced airway management if available. Continue nebulized albuterol en route. Transport emergently
Intubate the patient — this will bypass the mask seal problem
Stop BVM attempts and switch back to a non-rebreather mask — it was working better

critical.scenario: You are called to a skilled nursing facility for a 68-year-old female with altered mental status. The patient weighs approximately 380 lbs (BMI 50). She has a history of diabetes, hypertension, and a recent right hip replacement. The nursing staff reports she was confused this morning and her blood glucose is 52 mg/dL. She is awake but confused and cannot follow commands. The facility is on the third floor and has a standard elevator that measures 48 inches deep by 42 inches wide. The doorway to the patient's room is a standard 36-inch door. Your bariatric stretcher measures 32 inches wide and will fit through all doorways. The patient is currently lying in a standard hospital bed. The nursing staff has not attempted to move her. The patient's daughter arrives and is tearful, saying, 'Please be careful with her — she's so embarrassed about her weight and she hates when people stare.'
critical.question: This patient is hypoglycemic with AMS, needs IV dextrose or IM glucagon, and needs transport. There are equipment and dignity considerations. What is the EMS provider's best approach?
options: Treat the hypoglycemia immediately (IM glucagon since IV access may be difficult), then plan the move. Use a bariatric-rated stretcher. Call for additional personnel (4-6 people) for the transfer. Use a mechanical lift or slide board with a draw sheet to move the patient from the bed to the stretcher. Cover the patient with a sheet to maintain dignity. Be mindful of the daughter's request — speak respectfully, do not discuss the patient's weight. Treat the medical emergency first (hypoglycemia), then manage transport logistics
Treat the hypoglycemia and leave the patient at the facility since the staff should be able to manage hypoglycemia
Move the patient first, then treat the hypoglycemia in the ambulance
Explain to the daughter that the patient's weight makes transport difficult and the patient should be transferred by a specialty bariatric transport team
